lessons.title: Filter Fundamentals & Terminology
lessons.content:
A **filter** shapes a signal's spectrum, passing some frequencies and attenuating others. The four canonical responses are **low-pass** (passes below the corner), **high-pass** (passes above it), **band-pass** (passes a band), and **band-stop / notch** (rejects a band). An **all-pass** filter passes every frequency at unity magnitude but deliberately shifts phase.

The conventional boundary is the **cutoff** or **corner frequency**, defined at the **-3 dB** point: the frequency where output power falls to half the passband, i.e. voltage drops to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\approx 0.707$ of its passband value, since $10\log_{10}(0.5)\approx -3\,\text{dB}$.

Three regions describe the response. The **passband** is delivered with acceptable attenuation; the **stopband** is suppressed to the required level; the **transition band** between them is where the response rolls off. The steepness of that roll-off is governed by **filter order** $n$: each pole contributes about $-20\,\text{dB/decade}$ (equivalently $-6\,\text{dB/octave}$) of asymptotic slope, so $n$ poles give $-20n\,\text{dB/decade}$.

Some filter families trade flatness for steepness and so exhibit **ripple** — a bounded amplitude variation (in dB) inside the passband (Chebyshev) or in both bands (elliptic). For a radar tech, the mental model is familiar: this is just $H(j\omega)$ shaping, the analog precursor to the same pole-zero thinking used in DSP. Everything downstream in this module — topologies, approximations, anti-aliasing — builds on these terms.

items.front: Name the four primary filter responses by what they pass.
items.back: Low-pass (passes below $f_c$), high-pass (passes above $f_c$), band-pass (passes a band between two corners), band-stop/notch (rejects a band). An all-pass passes all frequencies but shifts phase.
items.hint: Think about which side of the cutoff survives.

items.front: What is the -3 dB point and why is it the conventional cutoff?
items.back: The frequency where output power drops to half (voltage to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\approx 0.707$) of the passband, since $10\log_{10}(0.5)\approx -3\,\text{dB}$. It marks the passband/transition boundary and is unambiguous to measure.
items.hint: Half power.

items.front: Define passband, stopband, and transition band.
items.back: Passband: frequencies passed with acceptably low attenuation. Stopband: frequencies attenuated to the required level. Transition band: the region between them where the response rolls off.
items.hint: Pass, reject, and the slope between.

items.front: How does filter order relate to roll-off slope?
items.back: Each order (pole) adds about $-20\,\text{dB/decade}$ ($-6\,\text{dB/octave}$) of asymptotic roll-off. So $n$th order gives $-20n\,\text{dB/decade}$: 1st = $-20$, 2nd = $-40$, etc.
items.hint: 20 dB/decade per pole.

items.front: What is passband ripple?
items.back: Bounded amplitude variation (in dB) within the passband caused by certain approximations (Chebyshev, elliptic). It is the price paid for a steeper transition at a given order.
items.hint: Wiggle before the cutoff.

items.prompt: A single-pole RC low-pass filter has what asymptotic roll-off far above its cutoff?
items.explanation: One pole gives $-20\,\text{dB/decade}$ (equivalently $-6\,\text{dB/octave}$). The slope scales by $-20\,\text{dB/decade}$ per additional order.

items.prompt: At the -3 dB cutoff frequency, the output voltage relative to the passband is approximately:
items.explanation: $-3\,\text{dB}$ is half power; voltage $= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\approx 0.707$ of the passband value, since power $\propto V^2$.

items.prompt: Which filter type removes a single interfering tone (e.g. 60 Hz hum) while passing everything else?
items.explanation: A narrow notch (band-stop) rejects a tight band around the offending frequency while leaving the rest of the spectrum intact.

items.prompt: What is the defining property of an all-pass filter?
choices: Flat magnitude across all frequencies, but frequency-dependent phase
items.explanation: An all-pass leaves magnitude unchanged but alters phase/delay versus frequency, useful for phase equalization and delay correction.

lessons.title: Passive Filters & Loading
lessons.content:
**Passive filters** use only R, L, and C — no power, no gain. The workhorse single-pole RC has corner $f_c=\frac{1}{2\pi RC}$, identical whether wired low-pass (output across $C$) or high-pass (output across $R$). Its inductive cousin gives $f_c=\frac{R}{2\pi L}$. At the corner the reactance equals the resistance, so the response is down $3\,\text{dB}$ and the phase shift is $45^{\circ}$.

**LC filters** combine two reactive elements and so realize true 2nd-order responses with resonant peaking and sharp roll-off *without resistive loss*, reaching high $Q$. They dominate at RF, where inductors are small and practical. At audio and below, inductors become bulky, lossy (winding resistance kills $Q$), and act as antennas — which is exactly why active RC filters displaced them there.

The core weakness of passive RC is **loading**. The output is a frequency-dependent voltage divider with a non-zero source impedance, so any downstream input impedance draws current, shifts the divider ratio, drops gain, and moves $f_c$. Worse, you cannot simply stack two RC sections to get a clean 2nd-order filter: the second section loads the first, the poles interact, and the combined response is *not* the product of two ideal stages. The fix is **isolation** — insert a unity-gain buffer (high $Z_{in}$, low $Z_{out}$) between stages so their transfer functions multiply cleanly. That buffering instinct is precisely what the op-amp brings to the active filters in the next lesson.

items.front: Give the cutoff formula for a simple RC filter.
items.back: $f_c=\frac{1}{2\pi RC}$. Same formula whether wired as low-pass (output across $C$) or high-pass (output across $R$).
items.hint: $2\pi RC$ in the denominator.

items.front: Give the cutoff formula for an RL filter.
items.back: $f_c=\frac{R}{2\pi L}$. The inductor's reactance equals $R$ at cutoff. RL filters are uncommon at low frequencies because inductors are bulky, lossy, and pick up interference.
items.hint: $R$ over $2\pi L$.

items.front: What advantage does an LC filter have over an RC filter?
items.back: LC filters realize 2nd-order (and higher) responses with sharp roll-off and resonant peaking without resistive loss, and can reach high $Q$. They are common at RF, where inductors are small and practical.
items.hint: Reactive-only, resonant.

items.front: What is the loading problem with passive RC filters?
items.back: The next stage's input impedance draws current and shifts the divider ratio, lowering effective $f_c$ and reducing gain. Cascaded passive stages also load each other, so they interact rather than multiply cleanly.
items.hint: Downstream impedance steals current.

items.front: Why can't you simply cascade two RC low-pass sections for a clean 2nd-order response?
items.back: The second section loads the first, so the combined response is not the product of two ideal 1st-order stages — the poles interact, giving a sloppier transition than a properly designed 2nd-order filter. A buffer between them fixes this.
items.hint: Interaction between stages.

items.front: Sallen-Key low-pass schematic (looking ahead to active filters): one op-amp, two resistors, two capacitors with positive feedback through C1. Study how isolation removes the passive loading problem.
items.back: Review the diagram above.

items.prompt: An RC low-pass filter has $R=1.6\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C=100\,\text{nF}$. The cutoff frequency is closest to:
In a series RC fed by a source, taking the output across the resistor produces what kind of filter?
items.explanation: At low frequency the capacitor's reactance is high and blocks signal; at high frequency the cap is a short and the resistor sees the signal. Output across $R$ = high-pass.

items.prompt: Main practical reason inductors are avoided in low-frequency active filter designs:
choices: They are bulky, lossy, and pick up stray fields
items.explanation: At audio/low frequencies the required inductors are large, have significant winding resistance (loss / low $Q$), and act as antennas. Active RC filters synthesize the same response without them.

items.prompt: Best way to cascade passive RC sections so each behaves independently:
choices: Insert a unity-gain buffer (op-amp) between stages
items.explanation: A buffer presents high input impedance and low output impedance, isolating stages so their transfer functions multiply cleanly without loading interaction.

lessons.title: Active Filters & Sallen-Key Design
lessons.content:
An **active filter** wraps an op-amp around an RC network, adding gain and buffering. The op-amp supplies high $Z_{in}$, low $Z_{out}$ (no loading), can provide gain, and — crucially — lets feedback synthesize **complex pole pairs** (resonance, $Q$) *without inductors*. That is how you get sharp 2nd-order responses at audio frequencies.

The **Sallen-Key** topology is the classic 2nd-order section: one op-amp (often a unity-gain buffer), two resistors, two capacitors, with positive feedback through one capacitor. With equal components the corner is $f_c=\frac{1}{2\pi RC}$; in general $f_c=\frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1R_2C_1C_2}}$. A worked design for $f_c=10\,\text{kHz}$: pick $C=10\,\text{nF}$, then $R=\frac{1}{2\pi f_c C}=\frac{1}{2\pi(10^4)(10^{-8})}\approx 1.59\,\text{k}\Omega$. Set $R_1=R_2=1.59\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C_1=C_2=10\,\text{nF}$.

The second design knob is **stage $Q$**, which sets the sharpness and peaking of the pole pair. A maximally flat (Butterworth) 2nd-order section needs $Q=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\approx 0.707$; you reach a target $Q$ by skewing the $R$ or $C$ ratios or by adding gain. Higher $Q$ means more peaking near the corner and more time-domain ringing.

The **multiple-feedback (MFB)** topology uses the op-amp inverting with two feedback paths; it is the standard single-op-amp **band-pass** section and gives good stopband and $Q$, at the cost of signal inversion. Build higher orders by **cascading** 2nd-order sections — three sections give 6th order ($3\times2$) — assigning each its own $f_c$ and $Q$ from approximation tables.

items.front: What does an op-amp add that turns a passive RC network into an active filter?
items.back: Gain and buffering: high $Z_{in}$, low $Z_{out}$ (no loading), available gain, and feedback that synthesizes complex pole pairs (resonance / $Q$) without inductors.
items.hint: Isolation, gain, complex poles without L.

items.front: What is the Sallen-Key topology?
items.back: A 2nd-order active filter using one op-amp (often a unity-gain buffer), two resistors, two capacitors, with positive feedback through one capacitor. Popular for low-pass/high-pass because it is simple and low-sensitivity at moderate $Q$.
items.hint: Two R, two C, one op-amp, positive feedback.

items.front: For a unity-gain equal-component Sallen-Key low-pass, what sets the cutoff?
items.back: $f_c=\frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ (generally $f_c=\frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1R_2C_1C_2}}$). With equal parts the $Q$ is fixed; to hit a target $Q$ you skew $R_1/R_2$ or $C_1/C_2$ ratios or add gain.
items.hint: Same RC form as the simple filter.

items.front: Design a 2nd-order Sallen-Key low-pass for $f_c=10\,\text{kHz}$ (equal-component, unity gain).
items.back: Pick $C=10\,\text{nF}$. Solve $R=\frac{1}{2\pi f_c C}=\frac{1}{2\pi(10^4)(10^{-8})}\approx 1.59\,\text{k}\Omega$. Use $R_1=R_2=1.59\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C_1=C_2=10\,\text{nF}$; set ratios/gain for the desired $Q$.
items.hint: Choose C, solve for R, then set ratios/gain for Q.

items.front: What is the multiple-feedback (MFB) filter and when is it preferred over Sallen-Key?
items.back: An inverting op-amp section with two feedback paths forming a 2nd-order response (commonly band-pass or low-pass). Preferred for band-pass and where an inverting, higher-$Q$ section with good stopband is wanted; it inverts the signal.
items.hint: Inverting, two feedback paths, good for band-pass.

items.front: What is stage $Q$, and what does high $Q$ do?
items.back: $Q$ sets the sharpness/peaking of a pole pair. Higher $Q$ gives a sharper corner with more peaking (and more time-domain ringing). $Q=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\approx 0.707$ is the maximally flat (Butterworth) value for a single 2nd-order stage.
items.hint: Sharpness and peaking of the pole pair.

items.prompt: You need a 2nd-order Sallen-Key low-pass at $f_c=1\,\text{kHz}$ and pick $C=15\,\text{nF}$ (equal C). The equal $R$ value is closest to:
A single 2nd-order Butterworth (maximally flat) section requires a stage $Q$ of approximately:
items.explanation: $Q=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\approx 0.707$ gives the maximally flat magnitude with no passband peaking for a 2nd-order Butterworth.

items.prompt: Which topology inherently inverts the signal and is the usual choice for a 2nd-order band-pass section?
items.explanation: The infinite-gain multiple-feedback (MFB) section is inverting and is the standard single-op-amp band-pass, offering good $Q$ and stopband.

items.prompt: To build a 6th-order low-pass filter from 2nd-order sections, you cascade:
items.explanation: Order adds: three 2nd-order sections give 6th order ($3\times2$). Each section gets a specific $Q$ and $f_c$ from the chosen approximation tables.

lessons.title: Approximations, Phase & Anti-Aliasing
lessons.content:
A given filter order can be realized with different **approximations**, each a different compromise. **Butterworth** is maximally flat in the passband with moderate roll-off. **Chebyshev** trades passband ripple for a steeper transition at the same order. **Bessel** sacrifices roll-off for **linear phase / flat group delay** — the best time-domain behavior. **Elliptic (Cauer)** gives the steepest transition possible for a given order by allowing equiripple in *both* bands.

The phase axis matters as much as magnitude. **Group delay** is $\tau_g=-\frac{d\phi}{d\omega}$, the time delay of the signal envelope at each frequency. If $\tau_g$ varies with frequency, spectral components arrive at different times, smearing pulse edges and causing intersymbol interference. Constant group delay = linear phase = preserved waveform shape — which is why **Bessel** is the right call for pulsed and radar/data paths, even though Butterworth has the flatter magnitude. Chebyshev and elliptic ring and overshoot.

For a single interfering tone, a **twin-T notch** (two RC T-sections) places a deep null at $f_c=\frac{1}{2\pi RC}$. **Switched-capacitor** filters emulate a resistor with a rapidly switched capacitor, making $f_c$ **clock-tunable** and fully integrable, at the cost of clock feedthrough.

The non-negotiable application is the **anti-aliasing filter** before any ADC. Sampling folds energy above the Nyquist frequency $\frac{f_s}{2}$ irreversibly back into baseband as aliases. An analog low-pass must attenuate above-Nyquist content *before* sampling — once aliased, it cannot be removed. **Oversampling** (sampling well above $f_s/2$) widens the transition band so a gentle analog filter suffices, with the sharp rejection done digitally before decimation.

items.front: Compare Butterworth, Chebyshev, Bessel, and elliptic by their headline tradeoff.
items.back: Butterworth: maximally flat passband, moderate roll-off. Chebyshev: steeper roll-off, passband ripple. Bessel: linear phase / flat group delay (best time-domain), gentlest roll-off. Elliptic/Cauer: steepest transition for a given order, ripple in BOTH bands.
items.hint: Flat / steep+ripple / linear-phase / steepest+both-band-ripple.

items.front: Overlay of magnitude responses for equal-order Butterworth, Chebyshev, and Bessel low-pass filters. Note Chebyshev's passband ripple and steep transition, Butterworth's flat passband, and Bessel's early, gentle droop.
items.back: Review the diagram above.

items.front: What is group delay and why does it matter for pulses and data?
items.back: Group delay $\tau_g=-\frac{d\phi}{d\omega}$ is the time delay of the signal envelope at each frequency. If it varies with frequency, spectral components arrive at different times, smearing pulse edges and causing intersymbol interference.
items.hint: Derivative of phase; constant = no smearing.

items.front: When should you choose Bessel over Butterworth?
items.back: When preserving waveform shape/timing matters more than magnitude sharpness: pulse/data paths, radar, audio transient fidelity — anywhere overshoot and phase distortion hurt. Butterworth wins when flat amplitude and reasonable roll-off matter and phase is secondary.
items.hint: Time-domain fidelity vs amplitude flatness.

items.front: What is a twin-T notch filter?
items.back: A passive RC network of two T-sections (one R-C-R, one C-R-C) producing a deep null at $f_c=\frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ with matched components. Often buffered or given positive feedback to make the notch deeper/tunable. Used to kill a tone like mains hum.
items.hint: Two T-shaped RC arms, deep null.

items.front: What is a switched-capacitor filter and its key advantage?
items.back: It emulates a resistor with a capacitor rapidly switched between nodes; effective $R$ (hence $f_c$) is set by the clock frequency. Advantage: $f_c$ is clock-tunable and accurate, and the filter integrates fully on-chip without precision R/C. Downside: clock feedthrough; needs anti-alias/reconstruction filtering.
items.hint: Clock sets the cutoff; R replaced by switched C.

items.front: Why MUST an anti-aliasing filter precede any ADC?
items.back: Sampling folds any energy above the Nyquist frequency $\frac{f_s}{2}$ back into baseband as aliases that cannot be removed afterward. An analog low-pass before the ADC attenuates above-Nyquist content so aliases stay below the noise floor. Otherwise the samples are corrupted irreversibly.
items.hint: Aliases are unrecoverable once sampled.

items.front: How does oversampling relax the anti-aliasing filter requirement?
items.back: Sampling well above Nyquist pushes $\frac{f_s}{2}$ far from the band of interest, widening the transition band so a gentle (lower-order) analog filter suffices. The sharp rejection is then done digitally, after which the data is decimated — trading ADC speed for cheaper analog filtering.
items.hint: More space between band edge and fs/2.

items.prompt: Which filter family gives the steepest transition for a given order, accepting ripple in both passband and stopband?
items.explanation: Elliptic/Cauer filters achieve the steepest transition for a given order by allowing equiripple in both bands. Chebyshev ripples in only one band; Butterworth and Bessel have none.

items.prompt: You are filtering a stream of digital pulses and must avoid overshoot and timing smear. Best approximation:
items.explanation: Bessel has nearly linear phase / flat group delay, preserving pulse shape and timing with minimal overshoot. Chebyshev and elliptic have nonlinear phase and ringing that smear pulses.

items.prompt: An ADC samples at $f_s=100\,\text{kSps}$. Aliasing corrupts the band of interest if significant signal energy exists above:
items.explanation: The Nyquist frequency is $\frac{f_s}{2}=50\,\text{kHz}$. Energy above 50 kHz folds into baseband as aliases, so the anti-aliasing filter must attenuate content above 50 kHz.

items.prompt: Constant group delay across the passband means:
choices: The magnitude response is perfectly flat
All frequency components are delayed equally, preserving waveform shape
There is no attenuation at any frequency
items.explanation: Constant group delay = linear phase, so every spectral component is delayed by the same time and the waveform shape is preserved. This is Bessel's strength and is unrelated to magnitude flatness.

items.prompt: A switched-capacitor filter's cutoff frequency is primarily tuned by:
items.explanation: The switched capacitor emulates a resistor whose value depends on the switching clock, so $f_c$ tracks the clock frequency, making the filter electronically tunable. It still needs anti-alias filtering at its own clock rate.
